text: BUTTERWICK (West), a chapelry in the p. of Owston, Lincolnshire, 11 m. NE of Glandford-Bridge. Pop. 865.
BUTTERWORTH, a township in the p. of Salford, Lancashire, 3 m. E of Rochdale. Pop. 5,088.

BUTTES, an isolated mountain-ridge in Upper California, in the Sacramento valley, in about N lat. 39° 14′, 6 m. in length, alt. 2,690 ft. above sea-level. The name buttes is applied in California to isolated hills of singular conformation, which here and there rise from the face of extensive plains, and appear to have at one time formed islands, when the adjacent plains were the bottom of lakes.

BUTTES, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 20 m. SW of Neufchatel, in a deep valley branching off from the Val-de-Travers. The sun is not visible above the mountain-line, which encircles this plain, for three months in the year. Pop. (Protesinhabitants.
BUTTESBURY, a parish in Essex, 6 m. SSW of Chelmsford, on the river Wid. Area 1,440 acres. Pop. 221.

BUTTEVANT, a parish and market-town in co. Cork. Area of p. 11,583 acres. Pop. 5,042.—The t. is situated on the river Awbeg, 6 m. N of Mallow. Pop. 1,524. It is an old town, and its environs are rendered classic by the muse of Spencer, who wrote his Faery Queen in the vicinity of B. It gives the title of Viscount to the Barrymore family.
BUTTHARDT, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Unter-Franken-Aschaffenburg, 12 m. SSW of Wurzburg. Pop. 850.
BUTTIGLIERA, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 17 m. NW of Asti. Pop. 3,500. Its locality is celebrated for its wine.
BUTTINGTON, a parish in Montgomeryshire, on the Severn. Pop. 826.
BUTTLAR, a village of Saxe-Weimar, on the r. bank of the Ulster, 6 m. S of Vacha, and 20 m. SSW of Eisanach.
BUTTLE, a town of Sweden, in the island of Gothland, 20 m. SSE of Wisby.
BUTTLINGEN, a town of Hanover, in the prov. of Luneburg.
BUTTOLPHS, or BUTTULPH, a parish of Sussex, 15 m. SE of Steyning, bounded on the E by the Agur, which is here navigable. Area 910 acres. Pop. in 1841, 48.
BUTTON, a small island at the S entrance of the strait of Macassar, and to the SE of Pulo Laut, in S lat. 4°, and E long. 116° 30'.
BUTTON'S BAY, an indentation on the W side of Hudson's bay, to the W of Cape Churchill.
BUTTON'S ISLANDS, a group at the E entrance of Hudson's strait, to the N of Cape Chudleigh, in N lat. 60° 30', and W long. 65° 1'.
BUTT'S MOUNTAIN, or BEN LOMOND, an extensive ridge in Van Diemen's Land, in the district of Launceston, forming a curve between the N and S Esk. It rises in the centre to the height of 4,200 ft.

BUTTSTADT, a town of Saxe-Weimar, circle of Weimar-Jena, 11 m. NNE of Weimar. Pop. 2,060. It possesses a church, an hospital, and an academy; and has manufactories of soap, woollen fabrics, hosiery, lace, &c.; breweries, distilleries, dye-works and tanneries; and a considerable commerce in horses cattle, and grain.
BUTTYEN, or Bokony, a town of Hungary, in the comitat and 40 m. ENE of Arad, march of Boros Jeno, near the l. bank of the Feher-koros.
BUTUAN, or BATUAN, a town on the N coast of Mindanao, in the Philippine group, at the head of a deep bay, on a river of the same name.
BUTUCARAHI, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Sao-Pedro-do-Rio-Grande, rising in a mountainridge which separates the basin of the Rio Pardo from that of the Jacuhi, and flowing S to the Jacuhi.
BUTZ, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Aargau, 9 m. N of Aarau. Pop. (Catholics) 250.
BUTZBACH, a bailiwick and village of Hesse- Darmstadt, 10 m. S of Giessen. Pop. of b. 2,246. Linen and woollen stuffs, worsted stockings, leather, and shoes are manufactured here.
BUTZFLETH, a village of Hanover, in the prov. of Bremen, on the l. bank of the Elbe, 4 m. N of Stade. Pop. 1,340.

BUTZOW, a town of Mecklenburg-Schwerin, 18 m. SSW of Rostock, on the Warnow. Pop. 3,769. It has manufactories of woollens, soap, playing-cards, tobacco, and paper; and was formerly the seat of a university, which was conjoined with that of Rostock in 1789.
BUUREN, a town of Holland, in the prov. of Gelderland, 5 m. NW of Thiel.

BUVAN, an extraordinary cave in the prov. of Silhet in Bengal, in the great range of limestone hills known as the Kasia hills. It presents numerous narrow passages, and several splendid halls, encrusted with stalactites: the mountain appearing to be perforated in all directions like a honeycomb.
BUVANGE, a village of Belgium, Luxemburg, 15 m. W of Luxemburg. BUX

BUXAR, a town and fortress of India, in the prov. of Bahar, 58 m. ENE of Benares. A great victory was gained here by Major Monro over Shuja ud Dowlah and Cossim Khan in 1764, which confirmed the British in the possession of Bengal and Bahar. The town is large, and has several handsome mosques, and neat bazars. The fort is a small square, with a high rampart cased with turf, four circular bastions, a deep and wide ditch, a glacis, and a sort of lower fort extending to the Ganges.

BUXEDWAR, or BAKSHIDWAR, a village and pass in Butan, 80 m. N by E of Rungpur in Bengal. in N lat. 26° 52'. The village is singularly placed on a ledge of rock open only to the S; and the pass might easily be made impregnable.
BUXEE. See BUKSHEE.
BUXERULLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meuse, cant. of Vigneulles. Pop. 264.
BUXHALL, a parish in Suffolk, 4 m. W by S of Market-Stow. Area 2,120 acres. Pop. 533.

BUXHEIM, or Buchseim, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Schwaben-Neuburg, 3 m. from Memmingen, on the Buxach. Pop. 380. An ancient Carthusian monastery here is now the seignorial castle of the counts of Waldbott-Bassenheim.
BUXIERE-LA-GRUE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Allier, cant. of Bourbon-l'Archambault, on the Morgon. Pop. 1,707.
BUXIERES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meuse, cant. of Vigneulles. Pop. 557.
BUXTED, a parish in Sussex, 1½ m. NE of Uckfield, on a branch of the Ouse. Area 7,020 acres. Pop. 1,574.
BUXTEHUDE, a town of Hanover, in the landrost and 12 m. SE of Stade, on the Este, 4 m. above its junction with the Elbe. Pop. 2,200. It was early a place of some trading importance.

BUXTON, a market-town and chapelry in the p. of Bakewell, co. of Derby, 36 m. NW by N of Derby, and 160 m. NW by N of London, on the high road from Derby to Manchester, near the source of the Wye, at an alt. of between 900 and 1,000 ft. above sea-level, in a valley surrounded by bleak elevated tracts of moorland. Area of chapelry, 540 acres. Pop. in 1801, 760; in 1841, 1,569. The upper part of B. is a Derbyshire village; the lower, in the elegance of its buildings, its show, and its parade, approximates to Bath. The Crescent, erected in 1781 by the Duke of Devonshire, is a fine range of buildings in the Grecian style. At the E extremity of this crescent are two hot baths supplied from Bingham's well, temp. 81°. Fronting the crescent is a rising ground, planted with trees, and laid out in shrubberies and walks. St. Anne's well, near the crescent, enclosed within a handsome building in the style of a Grecian temple, is the resort of those who drink the waters, which are conveyed thither into a marble basin, both hot and cold water being obtained within twelve inches of each other. The water is pleasant to the taste, and has a clear and sparkling appearance. The active ingredients in the B. water appear to be the hydrochlorate of soda, and carbonate of lime. Some part of the stimulating properties of the Buxton water may perhaps be imputed to the mere quantity of fluid taken into the stomach, for the water itself is but weakly impregnated. "Medicinally these waters seem entitled to the appellation of a mild saline mineral: they are perfectly pellucid; and, owing very probably to the presence of azotic gas, they are devoid of that vapid taste so observable in common water when heated to the same temperature." The B. season commences in June, and ends in October. The number of visitors varies from 10,000 to 12,000 annually.—About 3 m. SW of the town is Poole's hole, a large dreary cavern under Coltmoss hill, abounding with stalactites representing various natural forms. Very beautiful walks have been formed along the banks of the Wye, which is here a small stream; beyond these extends what is called the Duke's drive, forming a circuit of nearly 3 m. round the town.—Axedge, one of the highest summits in the peak of Derbyshire, is generally visited from B. It is about 3 m. from the town, and rises 1,000 ft. above the level of the Crescent.-Half-a-century ago, B. was described as being situated amidst the most dreary and cheerless scenery in the district of the Peak. The government of B. is vested in a constable; and the trade consists chiefly in the manufacture and sale of ornaments in alabaster, spar, and other minerals of the Peak.
BUXTON, a parish in Norfolk, 4 m. NW of Coltishall. Area 1,460 acres. Pop. 713.
BUXTON, a township in York co., in the state of Maine, U. S., 65 m. SW of Augusta, skirted on the SW by Saco river. Pop. 2,688.
BUXWAH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Allahabad, 20 m. NW of Hattah.

BUXY, a canton, commune, and town of France. in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, arrond. of Chalon-sur- Saone.—The cant., comprising 31 com., had a pop. of 15,461 in 1831.—The com. and town, 12 m. SW of Chalon, had a pop. in 1841 of 1,954. Good red and white wines are grown in this canton.

BUYUKBETCHIK, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Macedonia, sanjak and 27 m. E of Salonika, on the N bank of Lake B., and near the S base of Mount Betchik. It contains thermal springs and baths. Lake B., the ancient Bolbe, is 14 m. in length, and from 6 to 9 m. in breadth. It averages 26 ft. in depth, and abounds with fish, but becomes to a great extent dry in summer. A river issues from its eastern extremity, and after a course of 6 m. falls into the gulf of Contessa. BUYUK-DERE'H, BUYAKDERE, OF BUJUKDERE', [i. e. 'the Great valley'], a village of Turkey, or rather a series of villas, which derives its name from its site in a beautiful narrow plain, stretching between the hills and the sea around a small gulf at the mouth of the Bosporus, and opposite Therapia. There is excellent anchorage in the bay of B. The corps diplomatique, with their dragomans and dependents, have their summer-retreats here, extending nearly 2 m. along the water's edge, amidst gardens glowing with roses, orange flowers, jessamines, and all the luxuriance of southern vegetation.
BUYUK-KAINARDJI, a town of Turkey, in the sanjak and 15 m. SSE of Silistria.

BUYUK-CHEKMEDJI, a village of Turkey, in the sanjak of Gallipoli, at the entrance of a small bay of the same name, on the coast of the sea of Marmora, 18 m. W of Constantinople. It contains about 300 houses. Soliman I., in 1596, threw a bridge of 26 arches across the narrow mouth of this bay.

BUZANÇAIS, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Indre, and arrond. of Châteauroux. The cant. comprises 10 com., and in 1831 contained a pop. of 13,729. The town is situated, 15 m. NW of Châteauroux, on the Indre, the branches of which are here crossed by 5 bridges. Pop. 4,416. It has a considerable commerce in wool, and in the environs are some extensive iron-works.

BUZANCY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, arrond. of Vouziers. The cant. comprises 21 com., and in 1831 contained a pop. of 8,998. The town is 13 m. E of Vouziers. Pop. 925.

BUZAS-BOCSARD, a village of Transylvania, in the jurisdiction of Unter Alba. It contains a castle belonging to the counts of Bethlen. The environs afford wine, and possess productive saline springs. BYF
BUZEMA (AL), a town of Marocco, in the district of Rif Garet, 25 m. E of Penon.
BUZEMS, a village of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, and cant.of Severac-le-Chateau, 27 m. N of Milhau, and the same distance E of Rodez. In the environs is a mine of antimony.
BUZENIN, a town of Poland, in the circle and 40 m. SE of Kalisz, obwod and 11 m. SSE of Sieradz, on the l. bank of the Warta. Pop. 267. It contains 2 churches.

BUZET, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Garonne, cant. of Montastruc, on the l. bank of the Tarn, 20 m. NE of Toulouse. Pop. 1,258.— Also a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Lot-et-Garonne, cant. of Damazan, 8 m. N of Nerac. Pop. 1,617. The environs afford good red wine, and the fine white wines distinguished by the name of the vins pourris.
BUZIOS (CAPE), a promontory of Brazil, on the coast of the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro, in S lat. 22° 46' 3", forming the S boundary of the bay of San Salvador.
BUZIOS, a group of three islets off the coast of Brazil, of which the SE is in S lat. 23° 44′ 27″, W long. 44° 57′ 11".

BUZZARD'S BAY, an arm of the sea on the S coast of Massachusetts, U. S., 30 m. in length, and 7 m. broad. It has been proposed to carry a canal from the head of this bay across Cape Cod into Barnstable bay—a distance of only 8 m.—which would save the tedious navigation round the long sandy cape. The highest point of intervening ground is only 331 ft. above low water.
BWAMOLUMBA, a town and port on the Ebonji branch of the Jamur river, in W Africa.

BYAM MARTIN'S ISLAND, one of the North Georgian group, in N lat. 75° 20', W long. 104° 20', between Bathurst island and Melville island.—Also an island of coral formation in the Pacific, in S lat. 19° 40′ 22″, W long. 140° 22′ 28″. It is of an oval shape, 33 m. in its largest diameter.
BYAM MARTIN (CAPE), a boldly outlined and soaring promontory on the W coast of Baffin's bay, at the entrance of Lancaster's strait, in N lat. 73° 33', W long. 77° 10'.
BYANS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Doubs, cant. of Boussieres. Pop. 733.

BYANSE, a pass leading from the Bhot district of Kumaon into Tibet; the least in elevation of these passes, which are 5 in number. The path ascends to this pass along the upper part of the Kali, a branch of the Gogra; and the crest of the pass is about 15,000 ft. above sea-level.
BYBERRY, a township in Philadelphia co., in the state of Pennsylvania, on Poquessing creek, 113 m. E by S of Harrisburg. Pop. 1,055.
BYCHAWA, a town of Poland, in the obwod and 13 m. S of Lublin. Pop. 800.
BYCHOK, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Voronets, district of Pavlovsk, and 11 m. NW of Bogoutchar.
BYCHTON, a township in Whitford p., Flintshire. Pop. 676.
BYDELL, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, 20 m. W by S of Dinajepur.
BYDZOW. See BIDSCHOW.
BYER'S GREEN, a township in the p. of St. Andrew's Auckland, in the co. of Durham, 4 m. NNE of Bishop-Auckland, intersected by a branch of the Clarence railway. Area 790 acres. Pop. 489.
BYERSVILLE, a village in Sparta township, Lemington co., New York, U. S., 249 m. W of Albany.
BYFIELD, a parish in Northamptonshire, 7 m. SW by S of Daventry. Area 2,760 acres. Pop. 1,079.
BYFIELD, a village in Essex co., in the state of Massachusetts, U. S., 35 m. N by E of Boston, on Parker river.
BYFLEET, a parish in Surrey, 3 m. WNW of Cobham, intersected by the London and Southampton railway. Area 2,060 acres. Pop. 672.
BYFORD, a parish in Herefordshire, 7 m. WNW of Hereford, on the N bank of the Wye. Area 910 acres. Pop. 236.
BYGONBANY, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, 75 m. N by E of Dacca, in N lat. 24° 46'.
BYGRAVE, a parish in Hertfordshire, 2 m. NE by N of Baldock. Area 1,860 acres. Pop. 154.
BYHER, a township in the p. of All Saints, <math>1\frac{1}{4}</math> m. E of Newcastle-upon-Tyne. Pop. 6,024. There are large glass-works and potteries here.
BYKHOV (Novo), a town of Russia, in the gov. of Moghilev, on the r. bank of the Dnieper, 15 m. S by E of Bykhov-Staroi.

BYKHOV-STAROI, a town of Russia, cap. of a district in the gov. and 30 m. S of Moghilev, on the r. bank of the Dnieper. Pop. 3,900. It is surrounded by an earthen rampart and ditch, and defended by two large towers over the gates. It contains 2 Russian and 2 United Greek churches, a synagogue, several convents, and a castle which formerly belonged to the Sapieha family. This town was built by the hetman of Lithuania, Sapieha, in the reign of Sigismond.
BYKLE, a town of Norway, in the dio. of Nedena, 85 m. NNW of Christiansand, on the r. bank of the Torrisdal.
BYLAN. See BEILAN.
BYLAND-ABBEY, or BYLAND-CUM-MEMBRIS, a township in the p. of Coxwold, N. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. WSW of Helmsley. Area 3,130 acres. Pop. in 1841, 97.
BYLAND OLD, a parish in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. WNW of Helmsley, on the Rye. Area 3,120 acres. Pop. in 1841, 85.
BYLAUGH, a parish of Norfolk, 51 m. NE of Dereham, on the Wensum. Area 1,580 acres. Pop. in 1841, 85.
BYLEY-CUM-GATEHOUSE, a township in the p. and 2 m. N of Middlewich, Cheshire, near the Trunk canal. Area 820 acres. Pop. in 1841, 149.
BYNG MOUNT, a mountain of SE Australia, at the extremity of a branch of Balloon or W Taylor range, and 30 m. S of the Lachlan. It is of granitic structure, and scantily wooded.
BYNOE (Point), a headland on the coast of Western Patagonia, in the entrance of Fallos channel, with a group of islets extending for 2 m. off it, in S lat. 47° 57'.
BYNWESTON, a township in the p. of Worthen, Salop. Pop. in 1841, 91.
BYORAM, or BYARUM, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 130 m. ENE of Hydrabad, and 35 m. NNE of Kummummett.
BYQUA, or Boquan, a town of Upper Guinea, cap. of a district in the kingdom of Ashantee, 27 m. S of Komassi.
BYRA, or BEERA, a fort of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bahar, and zemindary of Chuta Nagpur, 40 m. SSE of Burwa.

BYRAGUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Gundwana, 90 m. SW of Sumbhulpur, on the N bank of the Kobragur, in N lat. 20° 18', E long. 82° 55'. In 1794 it contained 300 thatched and tiled houses, and a stone fort; and possessed a considerable trade, chiefly in cotton from the interior. It formerly belonged to Chanda.

BYR ALY, a village of Arabia, in the prov. of Hedjaz, a little to the W of Medina. The caravans from Damascus stop here before entering the town BYS 142 of the prophet, and here pilgrims to Mecca provide themselves with water.
BYRAMGHAUT, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Oude, on an inosculation of the Gogra, 35 m. NE of Lucknow.

BYRAM, a river of the United States, which runs along the SW confines of the state of Connecticut, and falls into Long island sound.—Also a township of Sussex co., in the state of New Jersey, bordered on the W by the Musconetcony river. It is generally hilly. Pop. in 1841, 1,153.
BYRAN DROOG, a town and port of Hindostan, in the prov. of Mysore, on an affluent of the Shinsha, 30 m. WNW of Bangalore, and 57 m. NE of Seringapatam.
BYRATH, a pass of North Hindostan, in the prov. of Gurwal, 75 m. ENE of Umbala, at an alt. of 7,599 ft. above sea-level.
BYRD, a township of Cape Girardeau co., in the state of Mississippi, U. S. Pop. in 1840, 2,575.
BYR-EL-BAR, or BEREMBA, a village of Upper Egypt, in the prov. of Thebes, 9 m. SSE of Gheneh, 3 m. from the E bank of the Nile, at the entrance of the valley of Kossier, on the caravan road to Mecca.

BYRESE, a town in the Great Oasis of Africa, 33 m. S by E of Boulak. Pop. 600. It is prettily situated on an eminence, one side of which is clothed with palm-trees, the other sides are surrounded by fields of grain, chiefly dourah.
BYRNVILLE, a village of Fulton township, in Schoharie co, in the state of New York, U. S., consisting of 12 or 15 dwellings.
BYROME, or BYRAM with Pool, a township in the p. of Brotherton, W. R. of Yorkshire, 13 m. NNW of Ferry Bridge, on the Aine, and close to the York and Derby railway.

BYRON, a town of Oxford co., in the state of Maine, U. S., 56 m. SW of Augusta. Pop. in 1840, 219.—Also a township in Genesee co., in the state of New York, 10 m. NE of Batavia. It is drained by Black creek, which affords good water-power, and has several sulphur springs. The surface is generally level, and the soil fertile and well-cultivated. Pop. 1,907.
BYRON CAPE, a headland of SE Australia, in S lat. 28° 35'.
BYRON CAPE, a promontory of the island of Santa Cruz, in Queen Charlotte's group, S Pacific, in S lat. 10° 40′, and E long. 166° 10′.

BYRON'S ISLAND, one of the Bishops group, in the S Pacific, in S lat. 1° 18', and E long. 178°. It is about 14 m. in length, and its surface, which is level, is covered with wood, in which the cocoa-nut tree predominates. The inhabitants are tall and wellformed. This island was discovered by the navigator whose name it bears in 1765.
BYRON ISLAND, the easternmost of the Guaian group, on the W coast of Patagonia, in S lat. 47° 54' 50", and W long. 75° 24' 32".
BYRON'S BAY, an indentation of the SE coast of Labrador, to the N of Ivucktoke or Hamilton inlet, in N lat. 54° 30′, and W long. 57° 30′.
BYRPOUR, or BEERPOOR, a small town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Gujerat, 60 m. ENE of Ahmedabad.
BYSAN. See BISAN.
BYSHOTTEES. See Brandon.

BYSKEA, a town of Sweden, in the laen and 85 m. NNE of Umea, and 25 m. SSW of Pitea, at the mouth of the Byske Aa, which, after an ESE course of 140 m., falls into the gulf of Bothnia, in N lat. 64° 55'.
BYSTRITZ. See BISTRITZ.
BYSTRITSA, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 25 m. ENE of Vilna, on the l. bank of the Viliya.
BYTHAM (CASTLE), a parish of Lincolnshire, 5 m. SW of Corby, and E of the Great North Road. Area 3,960 acres. Pop. in 1841, 672.
BYTHAM (LITTLE), a parish of Lincolnshire, on the river Glen. Area 1,010 acres. Pop. in 1841, 311.
BYTHORN, a parish of Huntingdonshire, 61 m. NNW of Kimbolton. Area 1,510 acres. Pop. in 1841, 322.
BYTON, a parish of Herefordshire, 4 m. E of Presteigne, in Radnor, S of the river Lug, and near Shobden-hill-wood. Area 1,030 acres. Pop. in 1841, 172.

BYTOWN, a town of Upper Canada, cap. of Dalhousie district, situated in the NE corner of Nepean township, on the Ottawa, at the mouth of the Rideau canal, 328 m. from Toronto, 1273 m. from Kingstown, in N lat. 45° 25'. It is divided, by the Rideau canal, into Upper and Lower B., which are built on opposite sides of the valley, the former about \frac{1}{2} a m. higher up the river. A short distance above Upper B. are the Chaudiere or Kettle falls, below which a suspension bridge crosses from Upper to Lower Canada. The pop. of the two towns is about 7,000, who are chiefly engaged in the lumber-trade. The average breadth of the Ottawa, between B. and the St. Lawrence, is about half-a-mile. The Chaudiere falls The double sound of the letter C in English, and its numerous interchanges with other letters, will suggest to the reader the propriety of consulting articles under K, Q, S, or Z for words which, according to the orthography of some writers, commence with C, but which do not appear to have found a place in the present work under this letter.]
CAAMANO (CAPE), a cape on the W coast of N America, in Clarence strait, in N lat. 55° 29'.—Van- 1,500. couver.

CAASH, or Cassabur, an aghalik and town of Turkey, in Anatolia, on a stream which issues from the E base of the Massacytus, in the sanjak of Meis. In its neighbourhood are the ruins of the ancient Candyba.
CAB (EL), a village of Upper Egypt, 10 m. N of Edfu.
CABAAT, a Mahommedan village of Palestine, between Nablous and Nazareth, containing from 50 to 80 houses.—Buckingham.

CABABURI, a river of Brazilian Guayana, flowing into the Rio Negro, on the l. bank, under the meridian of 66° W, 4 m. below the v. of Maçarube, after a course of about 100 m. Its waters are muddy, but abound in fish, and the banks with game. It is about 150 yards wide at its confluence with the here. Rio Negro.
CABAÇA, or Cabasao, a town of Lower Guinea, in the kingdom of Angola, the cap. of the territory of Ginga, 80 leagues ENE of Fort Pungo-Andonjo, in N lat. 8°, E long. 20°.

CABAÇAL, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Mato-Grosso, an affluent of the Paraguay on the r., between Villa-Maria and the confluence of the Sipotuba. CAB are passed by a canal from Lake Chaudiere into the Rideau navigation.

BYTURNEY, or VAITURANI, a river of Hindostan, which takes its rise in the mountainous region of Gundwana, about 30 m. WSW of Ramgur, and runs S, under the name of the Coyle, through the district of Chuta Nagpur. Entering the district of Cuttach, it takes the name of the Byturney, receives the Salundy and several other tributaries, and after a total course of from 300 to 400 m., falls, under the appellation of the Domrah, into the bay of Bengal, 12 m. N of Palmyras point. This river ranks second in size in the prov. to the Mahanuddy.
BYTYN, a town of Prussia, in the prov. and 18 m. WNW of Posen.
BYWELL (St. Andrew's), a parish of Northumberland, 13½ m. W of Newcastle, on the Tyne, and intersected by the Newcastle and Carlisle railway. Area 3,680 acres. Pop. in 1841, 452.
BYWELL (St. Peter's), a parish of Northumberland, in Tindale ward, nearly 8 m. in length and 5 m. in breadth. Area 21,780 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,303; in 1841, 1,512.
BYZAPOOR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 40 m. E of Arungabad.
BZURA, a river of Poland, in the prov. of Mazow, which runs past Zgierz and Lowicz, and unites with the Vistula opposite Wgszogrod.
CABACEIRAS, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Parahiba, in the district of Villa-da-Rainha. Pop.
CABACEIRO, a small peninsular projection of the coast of Mozambique, which connects it with the mainland of the SE coast of Africa.

CABALABA, a river of British Guayana, an affluent of the Corentyn, on the r. It is about 100 yards wide at its mouth, but is considerably wider 6 m. above. In N lat. 4° 47' and W long. 57° 13' it rushes over a rocky granitic barrier, forming a cascade about 25 ft. in height, called Avanavero .- Schomburgk.
CABALARIA (CAPE), or NAUCELLES, a high and steep cape on the N coast of the island of Minorca, in N lat. 40° 7'.
CABALLAR, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 10 m. NNE of Segovia. There are thermal springs
CABALLERO (FERNAN), a village and pueblo of Spain, in the prov. of Ciudad-Real, in the partido of Piedrabuena, 15 m. NW of Ciudad-Real.
CABALLEROS (JEREZ DE LOS), a partido and town of Spain, in the prov. and 30 m. S of Badajoz, near the river Ardila. Pop. 5,628.
CABALLEROS (BARCAROTTA JEREZ DE LOS), a village and pueblo of Spain, in the prov. of Badajoz, partido of Jerez de los Caballeros.
CABALLO (Passo), the principal entrance to [ Matagorda bay, on the coast of Texas, in N lat. 28° 24'.
CABALLO (PUERTO), a small harbour, formed by a low sandy point to seaward, on the N coast of Guatimala, in N lat. 15° 40', 7 m. NE by E of Omoa.
CABALLOS, a small bight on the coast of Peru, at the mouth of the Nasca, 230 m. SSE of Lima.

CABANAS, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Cacerès, 25 m. SE of Truxillo, at the foot of the Sierra de Gudalape. Pop. 1,500.—Also a fishing-village in the prov. and 10 m. E of La Corogne, in Galicia, on the bay of Betanzos, at the mouth of the Canteira.
CABANAS (PUERTO DE), a harbour on the NW coast of Cuba, 4 leagues E of Bahia Honda, in N lat. 23° 2′ 20", W long. 82° 58′ 30".
CABANAS-DE-ESGUEVA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 30 m. S of Burgos, on the r. bank of the Esgueva.
CABANAS-DE-YEPES, a village of Spain, in the prov. and 24 m. E of Toledo.
CABANEROS, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 45 m. S of Leon.
CABANES, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 15 m. NE of Castellon-de-la-Plana. Pop. 1,800.
CABANILLAS, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Navarre, 4 m. E of Tudela, near the l. bank of the Ebro.—Also a town in the prov. and 24 m. S of of exports, C. contributed 6,094,000. Soria.

CABANNES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Bouches-du-Rhone, cant. of Orgon. Pop. 1,525. Mulberries are extensively cultivated in this com.— Also a commune in the dep. of Tarn, cant. of Murat. Pop. 1,282.
